phases.label: Tenant Onboarding
phases.sublabel: One-time setup performed by The Ray admin when a new state DOT is brought on. DOT users have no visibility into this workflow.
phases.note: DOT users log into their subdomain (e.g., penndot.corridor-iq.org) and land directly in their workspace. There is no workspace selector in the DOT-facing UI — that concept is handled here at the admin level.

admin.label: Log into Admin Portal
admin.desc: Internal CGS admin UI — not accessible to DOT users. Used exclusively for tenant lifecycle management.
items: Internal URL, not a subdomain of corridor-iq.org
Role: CGS Admin — system-wide access
Authentication via Azure AD (separate policy from DOT users)

api.label: Provision Tenant Schema
api.desc: On workspace creation, the API runs a migration to create the tenant-isolated schema in PostgreSQL.
items: Creates schema named for the tenant (e.g., schema penndot)
Runs FluentMigrator InitialMigration within the new schema
Tables: PROJECTS, SCENARIOS, RUNS, CUSTOMER_UPLOADS, DATASET_CONFIGS, etc.
Inserts DOT_WORKSPACES record in main DB

data.label: DOT_WORKSPACES row
data.desc: Central tenant registry in the shared main DB. This record drives all routing and isolation — it is the source of truth for what tenants exist.
items: state_code (unique)
subdomain (unique)
schema_name
portal_group_id
image_service_id
created_at

process.label: Portal Group Created
process.sublabel: Scoped to tenant; image service registered
process.desc: ArcGIS-level content scoping boundary. All content shared to this group is only accessible to group members.
items: Group = tenant boundary inside ArcGIS Enterprise
Service account manages membership
One Image Service per workspace registered to this group
No DOT users are ever direct Portal users

admin.label: Create DOT Workspace
admin.desc: Minimum config to bring a new state DOT online. This record drives subdomain routing and all downstream isolation.
items: state_code: 2-letter FIPS (PA, TX, etc.)
subdomain: penndot → penndot.corridor-iq.org
schema_name: tenant-isolated PostgreSQL schema
Custom DOT domains also supported (e.g., ciq.penndot.org)

api.label: Seed Layer Config
api.desc: Stores initial DATASET_CONFIGS rows in the new tenant schema from a default template.
items: Seeded from default config template per analysis type
Admin can customize before finalizing onboarding
WORKSPACE_LAYER_PERMISSIONS records created for default layers

admin.label: Configure Default Layers
admin.desc: Each state gets default analysis layers with derivation rules stored as DATASET_CONFIGS. These are the baseline for all scenario weight configurations.
items: Configured per analysis type: Solar, Overhead TX, Underground TX
Each layer has a derivation rule (rasterize, slope, distance, etc.)
Default weights set here — users override per scenario
Source data from state-level DEMs in the shared Azure Blob bucket

api.label: Register Portal Group
api.desc: Server-side ArcGIS REST call to create the tenant Portal group.
items: Uses ArcGIS REST API (server-side, not user-facing OAuth)
Returns portal_group_id stored on DOT_WORKSPACES
Group is linked to tenant image service (image_service_id)

admin.label: Provision ArcGIS Portal
admin.desc: Creates an ArcGIS Portal group that scopes content access to this tenant.
items: One Portal group per DOT workspace
Service account used for all API calls — DOT users never log into Portal
One Image Service registered per workspace via image_service_id
Group membership managed by .NET API

phases.label: Authentication
phases.sublabel: How a DOT user reaches their isolated workspace after navigating to their state's subdomain.
phases.note: All API requests carry the JWT. The .NET API enforces tenant isolation on every query — schema_name in the token routes all queries to the correct tenant schema. Users can never see data outside their DOT_WORKSPACE.

user.label: Navigate to Subdomain
user.desc: Each state DOT gets a custom subdomain. Users navigate there directly — no central tenant-selection page.
items: Examples: penndot.corridor-iq.org or ciq.penndot.org (custom domain)
DNS points to Azure Front Door
Subdomain extracted from Host header — sets tenant context

api.label: Azure Front Door
api.desc: Entry point for all traffic. TLS termination, WAF rules, subdomain routing.
items: TLS termination at edge
WAF: Web Application Firewall protection
Routes all *.corridor-iq.org subdomains to the same App Service
Custom DOT domains (e.g., ciq.penndot.org) also route here

data.label: Tenant Lookup
data.desc: Simple indexed query resolving the subdomain to a tenant record.
items: SELECT * FROM dot_workspaces WHERE subdomain = $1
Indexed on subdomain column
Returns schema_name used for all subsequent tenant queries

user.label: Log In
user.desc: Authentication via Azure AD or Portal OAuth. Identity provider configurable per tenant.
items: Azure AD: standard OAuth2 flow with the DOT's own AAD tenant
Portal OAuth: for users with existing ArcGIS credentials
Auth token exchanged for application JWT scoped to tenant

api.label: Resolve Tenant
api.desc: The API extracts the subdomain from the Host header and looks up the DOT_WORKSPACES record to establish tenant context.
items: Host: penndot.corridor-iq.org → subdomain = penndot
SELECT * FROM dot_workspaces WHERE subdomain = 'penndot'
Returns workspace_id, schema_name, portal_group_id
404 if subdomain not registered

user.label: View Dashboard
user.sublabel: Their projects only — no workspace picker
user.desc: User lands on their state's dashboard. No workspace selector visible — tenant context is transparent via subdomain + JWT.
items: Shows only this state's projects
Admin features (workspace management) not visible to regular DOT users
Nav: Dashboard → Projects → Scenarios → Runs

api.label: Issue JWT
api.desc: After validating user identity and tenant, issues a JWT with tenant context embedded. All subsequent API calls use this token.
items: Claims: user_id, tenant_id, workspace_id, role
schema_name in token routes all DB queries to correct schema
Standard expiry + refresh token flow

api.label: React App Loads
api.sublabel: Scoped to tenant; no workspace selector shown
api.desc: React initializes with tenant JWT. Workspace selection (visible in early mockups) is not shown to DOT users — handled transparently via subdomain.
items: Tenant context read from JWT — no URL param or UI selector
ArcGIS Maps SDK proxied through .NET API
All requests include Authorization: Bearer {jwt}
UI scoped to user role — Manager sees more than Viewer

phases.label: Project & Scenario
phases.sublabel: DOT users define the study area (Project) and configure the analysis parameters (Scenario) before running the model.
phases.note: Analysis types: Solar Panel, Overhead Transmission, Underground Transmission. Weights control the WRO model — each dataset layer gets a relative importance value stored as JSON on the scenario. Custom uploaded layers appear here once processing completes. Dependency (Rusty): UI permission enforcement requires the permissioning module Rusty is extracting from product hub into a standalone ElevateGeo NPM package — flagged as a blocker by Joe in the 4/13 standup. Needed this week or next to avoid retrofitting permissions across all UI components later.

user.label: Create Project
user.sublabel: Name + draw study area polygon on map
user.desc: A Project defines the geographic study area. Users draw a polygon on the ArcGIS map viewer.
items: Required: name + boundary polygon drawn on map
Boundary stored as GeoJSON
Multiple scenarios can exist within one project

api.label: Validate + Store Project
api.desc: API validates boundary geometry and writes to tenant schema.
items: GeoJSON polygon validated (winding order, closure, CRS)
Written to PROJECTS in tenant schema
project_id returned to frontend

data.label: PROJECTS row
data.desc: Stores project metadata and study area boundary.
items: id, name, description
boundary: GeoJSON polygon
workspace_id (FK → DOT_WORKSPACES)
created_by, created_at

user.label: Create Scenario
user.desc: A Scenario is one configuration of the suitability model within a project. Multiple scenarios allow side-by-side comparison.
items: Required: name + analysis type (Solar / Overhead TX / Underground TX)
Linked to parent project
Can be cloned from an existing scenario to compare alternatives

api.label: Store Scenario
api.sublabel: Links to project, analysis type set
api.desc: Creates SCENARIOS record linked to the project.
items: Inserts SCENARIOS row with project_id FK
analysis_type: solar | overhead_tx | underground_tx
Initial status: draft

data.label: SCENARIOS row
data.desc: All scenario configurations for a project.
items: id, name, analysis_type
project_id (FK)
weight_config (JSON)
status: draft | configured | running | complete

user.label: Configure Weights
user.desc: Core analysis setup. Users assign importance weights to each dataset layer. Weights drive the suitability model output.
items: Each layer gets a relative weight
Layers sourced from DATASET_CONFIGS for this tenant
Custom uploaded layers appear here once status = complete
Custom Suitability Modeler widget (CGS-built replacement for Esri's closed-source version)

api.label: Serialize Weights
api.desc: Weight config validated and stored as JSON on the scenario.
items: Validates layer weight references exist in DATASET_CONFIGS
Stores complete JSON weight map on SCENARIOS.weight_config

user.label: Save Scenario
user.sublabel: Ready to run or adjust later
user.desc: Scenario saved and visible in dashboard. Can run immediately or be adjusted later.
items: SCENARIOS.status = configured
Weight values locked as JSON
Run button becomes active
Scenario can be duplicated for variations

phases.label: Data Pipeline & Custom Uploads
phases.sublabel: Two distinct flows feed the raster services. Default state layers run through an annual pipeline (The Ray manages this). Custom user uploads are on-demand and async. Neither flow pre-clips rasters — clips are applied on the fly at render time via JSON.
phases.note: Key architectural point (Melissa, 4/13 standup): rasters are NEVER pre-clipped per project or scenario. One WRO service is created per state (defaults) or per custom layer set. The scenario's study area polygon is passed as a clip in JSON at render time — so one stored raster serves every project in that state. The clip JSON is read from the application database at rendering time. VM startup is triggered by the .NET API via Azure Compute API immediately after the upload record is created.

flows.label: Annual Default Pipeline
flows.description: Run 1–2x/year by The Ray (not user-triggered). Loads default state-wide layers, creates one WRO service per state.

admin.label: Trigger Annual Pipeline
admin.sublabel: Kick off data refresh for all states
admin.desc: The Ray runs this 1–2x per year to refresh the default state-wide layer data for all tenants. Not triggered by DOT users.
items: Initiated by The Ray operations team
Runs for every active DOT state in the system
Produces the default layer set that DOT users see as their baseline rasters
Uses configuration from DATASET_CONFIGS per state

process.label: Run Pipeline Scripts
process.desc: Python GP scripts process raw source data into Cloud Raster Format (CRF) for each state. Handles all the default analysis layer types.
items: Per-state source data: elevation, land use, slope, ROW proximity, etc.
Scripts convert to CRF and store in Azure Blob (imagery/{state}/default/)
One set of output rasters per state — not per user, project, or scenario
Runs against the shared Azure Blob bucket (imagery folder)

process.label: Create WRO Service
process.sublabel: One service per state — shared by all users & projects
process.desc: After rasters are ready, the pipeline publishes or updates a Weighted Raster Overlay (WRO) service in ArcGIS Portal for this state. This is the key service that drives analysis.
items: One WRO service per DOT workspace (per state)
Backed by the CRF rasters in Azure Blob (imagery/{state}/default/)
Service is shared across ALL projects and scenarios in that state
Clip is NOT baked in — applied at render time via JSON

process.label: Rasters → Azure Blob
process.desc: Default layer CRFs land in the shared Azure Blob bucket under the imagery/default path, organized by state.
items: Container: shared bucket (same bucket as custom uploads and run results)
Path: imagery/{state}/default/
One set per state; refreshed annually
WRO service reads directly from this path

flows.label: Custom User Upload
flows.description: DOT user uploads from the app UI. API streams the file to blob, creates a DB record, then triggers VM startup. Windows Service on the VM polls for pending records and kicks off processing.

user.label: Navigate to Data Uploads
user.desc: The user accesses the custom data upload section from within their workspace. This is scoped to their DOT workspace — they can only see and manage their own uploads.
items: Found in the Data Management section of the app sidebar
Lists existing custom uploads with status badges (pending / processing / complete / failed)
Upload button opens file picker
Only visible to users with Editor or Manager role

user.label: Select & Upload File
user.desc: DOT users can upload their own elevation or vector data to use in analysis alongside default layers.
items: DEM, GeoTIFF, vector formats (exact support TBD)
File streamed to Blob via API
User sees upload progress + status badge

api.label: Validate + Stream to Blob
api.desc: API validates the file and streams it to Azure Blob Storage (shared bucket, user-uploads path).
items: Format validation (extension + MIME type)
File size limits enforced
Streams to blob: user-uploads/{state_code}/{filename}
Returns blob path

data.label: Raw File Stored
data.desc: The shared Azure Blob Storage bucket stores all raw uploaded files, organized by state code under user-uploads/.
items: Container: shared bucket
Path: user-uploads/{state_code}/{upload_id}/{filename}
Private — not directly user-accessible
Retained for potential reprocessing

user.label: Layer Ready
user.desc: Once processing is complete, the user is notified and the custom layer becomes available in scenario configuration.
items: UI polls CUSTOMER_UPLOADS status (or WebSocket push — TBD)
Custom layer appears in scenario weight panel
Marked as user-provided (distinct from default layers)

api.label: Create Upload Record
api.desc: Creates a CUSTOMER_UPLOADS record to queue the job for the Processing VM.
items: workspace_id, filename, blob_path
status: pending
upload_type: dem | vector

data.label: CRF Written
data.desc: After processing, the Cloud Raster Format output is written to the imagery/ path in the same shared bucket and served by ArcGIS Image Service.
items: Same shared Azure Blob bucket, different folder
Path: imagery/{state_code}/custom/{upload_id}.crf
CRF: cloud-native format optimized for ArcGIS Image Service
Tenant-scoped by state_code in path

api.label: Start Processing VM
api.sublabel: Azure Compute API — start VM if not running
api.desc: After creating the CUSTOMER_UPLOADS record, the .NET API calls the Azure Compute API to start the Processing VM if it is not already running. This keeps the VM off when idle to reduce cost.
items: Calls Azure Compute API: Virtual Machines - Start
If VM is already running (another job in progress), call is a no-op
VM name / resource group stored in app config (per environment)
API does not wait for VM to boot — it fires and returns immediately

process.label: VM Boots
process.sublabel: Azure starts VM; Windows Service starts automatically
process.desc: Azure starts the Processing VM in response to the Compute API call. Once booted, the Windows Service that manages upload processing starts automatically.
items: Azure VM boot time: typically 1–3 minutes
Windows Service configured to start automatically on boot (Services panel)
Service immediately begins polling on startup
VM stays running until all pending records are processed, then idles / deallocates

process.label: Windows Service Polls DB
process.desc: Windows Service on the Processing VM periodically queries the database for uploads needing conversion.
items: SELECT * FROM customer_uploads WHERE status = 'pending'
Poll interval configurable (1–5 min)
VM can be started on-demand by .NET API when a new upload arrives
Processes uploads one at a time or in batch

process.label: Download Raw File
process.sublabel: Pulls from user-uploads/ in blob via SAS token
process.desc: The Windows Service reads the blob_path from the CUSTOMER_UPLOADS record and downloads the raw file from Azure Blob Storage using a SAS token before processing.
items: Reads blob_path from CUSTOMER_UPLOADS record
Generates or uses stored SAS token for the user-uploads/ container
Downloads to local VM temp directory for processing
Sets CUSTOMER_UPLOADS.status = processing before starting

process.label: Python: Convert to CRF
process.desc: Python GP code downloads the raw file from Azure Blob and converts to Cloud Raster Format. Passes through bucket credentials and tenant context.
items: Downloads from user-uploads/{state}/{filename} using blob SAS token
ArcPy / GDAL conversion to CRF
Parameters passed through: bucket, token, location (./dem-name), tenantId
CRF written to imagery/{state}/custom/

process.label: Publish Custom WRO Service
process.sublabel: ArcGIS Portal — clip applied on the fly
process.desc: When a user uploads custom layers, the VM processes the rasters AND publishes a custom WRO service for those layers. Clip still applied on the fly — not baked in.
items: Custom WRO service created for this tenant's custom layer set
Backed by CRF at imagery/{state}/custom/{id}.crf
Clip polygon still applied via JSON at render time (not stored per project)
Custom service registered alongside the default state service in ArcGIS Portal

process.label: Status → Complete
process.desc: Processing VM updates the record and signals that the layer is ready.
items: CUSTOMER_UPLOADS.status = complete
crf_blob_path stored on the record
VM Windows Service idles until next job
VM may be deallocated after idle timeout to reduce cost

phases.label: Analysis Run
phases.sublabel: User triggers a run on a configured scenario. The .NET API builds a runtime JSON payload containing the scenario's weight config and clip polygon (study area), then passes it to the WRO service. No rasters are clipped or stored per run — the clip is applied on the fly at render time.
phases.note: Architecture confirmed 4/13 standup (Melissa): the WRO service runs against the full state-level raster. The scenario's project boundary polygon is passed as a clip in JSON — the service applies it at render time. No per-run rasters are stored. Whether final scored outputs are persisted to blob for export is TBD. Clip is at the scenario level; the same clip JSON that drives the UI also drives the analysis.

user.label: Trigger Run
user.desc: User clicks Run on a configured scenario. Submitted asynchronously — user does not wait on the page.
items: Scenario must be status: configured
Status badge updates to: running
User can navigate away; run continues in background

api.label: Create Run Record
api.desc: Before submitting the GP job, the API creates a RUNS record to track status throughout the async process.
items: scenario_id, workspace_id
status: queued
started_at, submitted_by
Returns run_id to frontend for polling

data.label: RUNS row
data.desc: Each row is one execution of a scenario. Tracks full lifecycle from submission to completion.
items: id, scenario_id, workspace_id
status: queued | running | complete | failed
job_id: ArcGIS GP job identifier
started_at, completed_at, result_blob_path

process.label: WRO Service (per state)
process.sublabel: Always running; one per state for defaults
process.desc: A Weighted Raster Overlay service published in ArcGIS Portal for this state. Always running, shared across all users and projects in the state. One for default layers, additional ones for custom layer sets.
items: Always-on service — no startup time per run
Backed by imagery/{state}/default/ in Azure Blob
One service per DOT workspace (per state) for defaults
Custom layer uploads trigger creation of additional WRO services

user.label: Results Displayed
user.desc: When the GP job finishes, the run record is updated and the user is notified.
items: RUNS.status → complete
Notification shown in dashboard
Result rasters available in map viewer
Run duration and job_id recorded for audit

api.label: Build Runtime JSON
api.desc: The API reads the scenario's weight config and project boundary polygon, then builds the JSON payload that will be applied to the WRO service portal item at render time.
items: weight_config: from SCENARIOS.weight_config
clip_polygon: from PROJECTS.boundary (the study area the user drew)
Clip is at the SCENARIO level — same clip drives both analysis and visualization
JSON structure matches what ArcGIS portal item accepts for runtime overrides

data.label: Clip Source: SCENARIOS
data.sublabel: Boundary polygon read from scenario at render time
data.desc: The clip polygon is read from the application database at render time — it is not stored in ArcGIS or blob storage. The scenario's parent project boundary is the clip extent.
items: PROJECTS.boundary: GeoJSON polygon drawn by the user when creating the project
Passed into the runtime JSON payload at the time of each render
Same polygon used for both the clip and the visual project boundary overlay
Not pre-baked into any raster — applied fresh every time the map renders

process.label: Apply Runtime JSON
process.sublabel: Weights + clip polygon applied on the fly
process.desc: At render time, the API patches the WRO portal item with the runtime JSON containing the weight config and clip polygon. The service applies the clip on the fly — no raster is clipped or stored.
items: Clip polygon applied via JSON to the portal item (not a geoprocessing job)
Weight config applied — same service, different weights per scenario
User sees the result clipped to their study area, but the source raster is unchanged
Multiple users can query the same service simultaneously with different clip/weight params

api.label: Apply JSON to WRO Service
api.sublabel: Patch portal item with clip + weights
api.desc: The .NET API patches the ArcGIS Portal item for the state's WRO service with the runtime JSON payload. This sets the clip and weights for this render without a separate GP job.
items: PATCH to ArcGIS Portal item for the state's WRO service
Payload: { clip_polygon: <GeoJSON>, weights: <weight_config> }
No job_id returned — this is synchronous portal item update
WRO service immediately reflects new clip + weights on next tile request

process.label: Render Suitability Map
process.sublabel: User sees clipped result — no raster stored per run
process.desc: The WRO service renders the suitability output for the clipped area with the applied weights. The React frontend displays this as a tile layer in the map viewer.
items: Output: suitability score raster rendered as map tiles (1–9 scale)
Clip appears to user as if the data is specific to their study area
No per-run raster stored — the render is on-demand each time the map loads
Whether scored outputs are persisted for export is TBD (flagged in architecture meeting)

api.label: Run → Complete
api.sublabel: RUNS record updated; result URL returned
api.desc: After the WRO portal item is updated, the API marks the run complete and returns the service URL for display in the frontend.
items: RUNS.status → complete
Returns WRO service URL (scoped to tenant) to the React frontend
No blob result path — output is a live service render, not a stored file
TBD: whether a snapshot/export is stored to blob for download

phases.label: Results
phases.sublabel: Users view, compare, and export analysis outputs in the map viewer. All access to ArcGIS and blob storage is proxied through the .NET API.
phases.note: End users never call ArcGIS Enterprise directly. All requests are proxied through the .NET API using a non-interactive Portal service account (least-privilege). Blob access uses time-limited SAS tokens generated per request.

user.label: Open Run Results
user.desc: User navigates to a completed run. Map viewer loads result rasters as a layer overlay.
items: Run listed in project dashboard with status badge
Click run → opens map view with results
Suitability color ramp applied (1 = unsuitable, 9 = highly suitable)
Project boundary polygon shown as reference

api.label: Fetch Run Metadata
api.desc: API retrieves the RUNS record and generates signed SAS URLs for the result blobs.
items: GET /api/runs/{run_id}
Returns run metadata + signed SAS blob URLs (15–60 min expiry)
Validates user is authorized for this workspace
Blob URLs passed to React for map layer loading

data.label: Serve Result Rasters
data.sublabel: SAS token, scoped to tenant path
data.desc: Result rasters served via SAS tokens (time-limited). No direct public blob access.
items: SAS token generated by .NET API
Expiry: 15–60 minutes (configurable)
Tenant-scoped path: imagery/{state_code}/runs/
All access brokered by API — no public URLs

process.label: Image Service
process.sublabel: Serves mosaic dataset tiles to React map
process.desc: Serves mosaic datasets for both input data and processed outputs as tiled map layers.
items: One Image Service per DOT workspace (image_service_id on DOT_WORKSPACES)
Backed by Mosaic Dataset in ArcGIS Geodatabase (reads from shared Blob bucket)
Serves tiles to the React map viewer on demand
Access proxied through .NET API — users never call Image Service directly

user.label: Compare Scenarios
user.desc: Multiple scenario runs can be compared in the same map view.
items: Toggle between scenario results in the same map extent
Summary stats panel shows score distributions side-by-side
Useful for comparing different weight configurations
Swipe comparison widget (TBD)

api.label: Render in Map Viewer
api.sublabel: ArcGIS Maps SDK loads result rasters
api.desc: React uses ArcGIS Maps SDK to load the result raster as a map layer from the signed blob URL.
items: ImageryLayer pointed at signed URL or Image Service endpoint
Suitability color ramp symbolization applied
User can adjust opacity, toggle layers
Project boundary drawn as GraphicsLayer

user.label: Export / Share
user.desc: Users can download results or share a link to a specific run.
items: Download: signed blob URL for raster outputs
Share: permalink to run (requires auth on recipient side)
Export formats: TBD — likely GeoTIFF + GeoJSON for corridors
PDF map export via ArcGIS Maps SDK PrintTask (TBD)
